explanation: The phrase 'prop_0 and prop_1 to allow prop_2' suggests that for prop_2 to occur, both prop_0 and prop_1 must have occurred beforehand or are currently true; in other words, prop_2 is only allowed when both prop_0 and prop_1 hold. In LTL, we can encode this constraint as: it is always the case that if prop_2 holds, then both prop_0 and prop_1 also hold.

The statement requires that both prop_0 and prop_1 hold together in the RM mode in order to allow lung inflation at PRM. Assuming 'lung inflation at PRM' is an effect caused by both prop_0 and prop_1 being true (in other words, their conjunction must always be true in the desired scenario), the formula globally enforces their simultaneous truth.

The requirement implies that during the RM phase at the PRM state, both prop_0 and prop_1 must be true (to allow lung inflation). Since we're not given explicit definitions for 'RM' or 'PRM' as atomic propositions, and only have prop_0 and prop_1, the translation focuses on the combination of the two properties. The usual LTL way to express 'both must be set' is G(prop_0 & prop_1), but unless further context tells us when, an LTL formula for 'at the relevant time, both are true' is (prop_0 & prop_1).

The statement suggests that both prop_0 and prop_1 must occur (be true) in order to permit prop_2. Typically, this means that whenever prop_2 occurs, both prop_0 and prop_1 must have occurred first (or are true at that time). A strong reading is: 'prop_2 cannot occur unless both prop_0 and prop_1 are true.' In LTL, this is captured as globally, if prop_2 holds, then both prop_0 and prop_1 must hold at the same time: G(prop_2 -> (prop_0 & prop_1)).

The sentence requires that both prop_0 and prop_1 must occur to allow prop_2. Thus, prop_2 should only be allowed if both prop_0 and prop_1 have occurred (which can be captured by the past or a form of causality), but in standard LTL over infinite traces, we can express this as: globally, if prop_2 holds, then previously prop_0 and prop_1 both held. Since we're not allowed to use past operators, we constrain prop_2 to not happen until both prop_0 and prop_1 occur: (!prop_2) U (prop_0 & prop_1), then prop_2 can occur.

The phrase suggests that whenever both prop_0 and prop_1 are true, then prop_2 is allowed (that is, prop_2 can eventually occur). In LTL, 'allowed' usually means 'permitted at some point if the conditions are met,' so the formula enforces that if always when prop_0 and prop_1 hold, then prop_2 is permitted (eventually true).

The statement says that to allow prop_2, both prop_0 and prop_1 must hold. In LTL, this means that whenever prop_2 holds, prop_0 and prop_1 must also be true at the same time. Thus, globally, whenever prop_2 is true, prop_0 and prop_1 are both true.

The sentence asks for both prop_0 and prop_1 to allow lung inflation at PRM. Assuming 'at PRM' denotes a particular context or state (which has no atomic proposition in the list), the requirement is that during RM (which we have to simply encode as a constraint because we cannot specify numerical comparisons or context), both prop_0 and prop_1 must hold. So, it is most naturally translated into always globally (i.e., G) needing prop_0 and prop_1 to be true together.

The requirement seems to state that in the 'release module' (RM), 'prop_0' and 'prop_1' must occur (be true) to allow 'prop_2'. Interpreting this as: 'prop_2' can only happen if both 'prop_0' and 'prop_1' have previously occurred (or are currently occurring). In LTL, to express that 'prop_2' implies that both 'prop_0' and 'prop_1' have happened or are true, we use 'prop_2 -> (prop_0 & prop_1)'.

The sentence indicates that during 'RM' (Recruitment Maneuver), both 'prop_0' and 'prop_1' must be true to allow something (lung inflation) at 'PRM' (possibly another temporal or system condition). Since we're only given 'prop_0' and 'prop_1' as atomic propositions and are not modeling 'RM' or 'PRM' as atomic propositions, the LTL encoded meaning is that 'prop_0' and 'prop_1' must hold at the relevant time.
